title: Boerne
name: Boerne
metaDescription: 1031 exchange coordination for Boerne boutique retail, multifamily, and flex property in the Hill Country, with aquifer and zoning review handled ahead of identification.

sections.html: Boerne is the Kendall County seat on the I-10 corridor northwest of San Antonio, and its 1031 exchange activity runs mostly through boutique retail, low-rise multifamily, and small-footprint flex space rather than large industrial parcels. Investors moving proceeds into Boerne are usually trading affluence and limited supply for a smaller deal size, and the documentation has to account for Hill Country zoning overlays that don't apply closer to downtown San Antonio.

sections.heading: Boerne Asset Stock Along Main Street and the I-10 Corridor
sections.html:
Historic Main Street carries boutique retail and restaurant space aimed at both residents and Hill Country visitors, while newer retail along the I-10 frontage serves the county's growing residential base. Multifamily product tends to be low-rise given local height and density limits, and light industrial or flex space is scarce and tightly held.

Any of these asset types can serve as replacement property for an investor exiting real estate held for business or investment use elsewhere, since the like-kind standard doesn't require matching asset class or market.

Small medical and professional office has also grown along the I-10 frontage as Kendall County's population base has expanded, giving an exchange buyer another category to weigh alongside boutique retail and low-rise multifamily. Owner-user turnover along Main Street tends to be slow, so available listings there draw close attention when they surface.

sections.heading: What Drives Demand in Boerne
sections.html:
Population growth in Kendall County has outpaced retail construction in several Boerne submarkets, keeping vacancy low on well-located space. Hill Country tourism supports the Main Street retail base independent of local rooftop counts, and the area's reputation for strong schools and lower density continues to draw residents away from denser San Antonio submarkets.

Limited institutional-grade supply means Boerne trades at a premium relative to comparable product closer to downtown San Antonio, which changes the math on cap rate expectations for exchange buyers.

sections.heading: Identification and Closing Coordination for Boerne Property
sections.html:
Boerne's Edwards Aquifer recharge zone status and Hill Country tree-preservation ordinances can affect what a parcel is actually entitled to build or expand, so that review needs to happen before a property goes on a written identification. Coordination for a Boerne file typically covers:

  • Aquifer recharge zone and impervious cover restrictions specific to the parcel
  • City of Boerne or Kendall County tree-preservation and site-plan requirements
  • Written identification meeting the unambiguous-description standard
  • Lender preflight on smaller loan sizes typical of Boerne deal size
  • Title and survey items routed to the qualified intermediary before the exchange agreement is finalized

This is documentation coordination, not a substitute for the investor's own engineering, legal, or tax review.

sections.heading: Watchouts for Boerne Investors
sections.html:
Aquifer recharge zone limits can reduce the buildable footprint on an otherwise attractive Boerne parcel, and that detail sometimes surfaces only after a site plan is pulled, which is too late if the property is already your sole identification. Institutional-grade inventory is thin, so an investor working the 45-day window without a backup identification risks having no fallback if the primary deal falls through.

Premium pricing relative to other San Antonio-area submarkets also compresses achievable cap rates, which matters for debt-replacement and boot calculations.

Seasonal swings in Hill Country visitor traffic can also affect Main Street retail income more than a straight residential-retail model would suggest, so a lender's underwriting should account for that variability rather than annualizing a single strong quarter.

sections.heading: Qualified Intermediary Role for Boerne Replacement Property
sections.html: Given the thinner deal flow in Boerne, investors identifying property here are more likely to need the three-property or 200% identification rule to keep a backup option in place rather than relying on a single Main Street or I-10 parcel. The qualified intermediary's documentation and proceeds-holding role stays the same regardless of deal size, but earlier engagement helps when the available replacement inventory is limited. Investors should also plan to move quickly once a suitable Boerne property is located, since well-priced listings in this submarket tend to draw multiple offers before a written identification can be finalized.

faqs.q: Is Boerne retail property too small a deal size to use as 1031 replacement property?
faqs.a: No, there's no minimum deal size under the like-kind exchange rules; a smaller Boerne asset can fully or partially replace a larger relinquished property, though boot may apply to any value difference.

faqs.q: How does the Edwards Aquifer recharge zone affect a Boerne identification?
faqs.a: Recharge zone status can limit impervious cover and buildable area, which should be confirmed with the city or county before the property is named in your written identification rather than discovered afterward.

faqs.q: Should I use the three-property rule for a Boerne exchange given limited inventory?
faqs.a: Many investors do, precisely because Boerne's institutional-grade supply is thin; keeping two or three identified properties in reserve reduces the risk of having no fallback if your primary Boerne deal falls through.

faqs.q: Does Boerne's premium pricing change my boot exposure?
faqs.a: It can. Higher per-square-foot pricing relative to other submarkets affects how much debt and cash you need to replace to avoid boot, so your lender preflight and tax advisor conversation should use current Boerne pricing, not comparables from elsewhere in the metro.

faqs.q: Who reviews tree-preservation requirements on a Boerne parcel?
faqs.a: That review comes from the city or county planning department and the investor's own site engineer; our coordination role is tracking that the documentation is in hand before the identification deadline, not performing the engineering review.
